patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: fix defrag_mode for non-reclaimable allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where the defrag_mode in the Linux kernel's page allocator fails to handle non-reclaimable allocations, such as those made with GFP_ATOMIC flags. When defrag_mode is enabled, it enforces ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT to prevent fragmentation, but this causes failures for non-reclaimable allocations that cannot invoke direct reclaim or compaction. The patch clears the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT flag and retries the allocation if it requests kswapd reclaim but cannot do direct reclaim itself. This fix addresses a large number of SLUB allocation failures under network-heavy workloads.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that defrag_mode=1 causes SLUB allocation failures for GFP_ATOMIC allocations under network-heavy workloads, agreeing that the issue is significant enough to warrant a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
We observed it on a few of the Meta workloads that adopted
defrag_mode=1.

For the service under load there were 85509 SLUB allocation failures
messages in dmesg within 2 hours. All of them are GFP_ATOMIC allocations
for skbuff_head_cache, despite free pages being available in other
migratetype freelists (~13 GB free).

Since it is networking path from the practical point of view, this means
dropped packets, failed RPC requests, tail latency spikes and overall
service degradation.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that defrag_mode was originally developed to address higher order / THP success rates under pressure, but its implementation has structural issues, specifically that it relies on preproducing watermark buffers in contiguous pageblocks for higher orders, which can fail under competition. The reviewer expressed hope to make defrag_mode the default eventually and acknowledged some bugs and overreclaim issues still being figured out.
review_comments.raw_body:
As you may remember it was developed to help with higher order / THP
success rates under pressure.

The impetus for actually deploying it was that we saw issues with
avalanches of large page cache folios vacuuming up the higher-order
chunks; this (ironically) also led to failures on the network side.

It's kind of a structural problem. We have real preproduction buffers
for order-0 pages through the watermarks. But for higher orders we
only ensure there is at least one page. That easily fails under even
mild competition.

Since we wanted to roll defrag_mode for THP in multi-tenant systems
anyway, we figured we might as well take the plunge now and battle
test the feature this way.

defrag_mode fixes *that* issue, by preproducing watermark buffers in
contiguous pageblocks - making everything up to that order more
readily available. I'm still hoping to make it the default eventually,
which was the plan with the original huge page allocator series. As we
keep leaning into higher order requests more and more, and especially
grow the non-optional ones, we kind of need non-optional preproduction
guarantees for higher orders as well.

But there are bugs like this one, and we're still figuring out some
overreclaim issues with it in production as well. So I'm glad it's
optional for the time being ;-)

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks from unconditional guards in the Linux kernel. The checks were dead code because the guards guarantee a non-NULL lock pointer at destructor time. The patch affects several macros, including DEFINE_GUARD(), DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(), and DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_0(). It also fixes a few specific instances where the NULL check was not being used correctly. The changes are verified to have no performance impact, with a negligible reduction in code size.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch annotates guard constructors in the Linux kernel with __nonnull(), which tells the compiler to warn when a NULL pointer is statically known to be passed. This is done by re-declaring the constructor with __nonnull() for DEFINE_GUARD() and directly annotating it for __DEFINE_LOCK_GUARD_1(). The __nonnull() macro is defined in compiler_attributes.h, following existing conventions. This change aims to improve code quality by catching potential NULL pointer issues at compile-time.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a conflict between their __nonnull() macro and glibc's own macro, proposed three possible solutions: dropping the macro, renaming it to avoid conflict, or redefining it to match glibc's convention; they are leaning towards option 1.
review_comments.raw_body:
Seems like clang is not confused here, I was able to reproduce the problem
with GCC 11 as well.

There is a conflict with glibc's own __nonnull macro

    https://elixir.bootlin.com/glibc/glibc-2.43/source/misc/sys/cdefs.h#L560-L562

which doesn't match the one from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.
They usually don't collide, except for User Mode Linux builds, which
include both kernel and userspace headers.

Options are:

1. Drop the __nonnull() macro from include/linux/compiler_attributes.h
   and use __attribute__((__nonnull__())) directly in
   include/linux/cleanup.h. This is a bit unfortunate, since __nonnull()
   seems like a useful shortcut, but seems like the simplest solution.

2. Rename __nonnull() to __nonnull_args() to avoid the conflict.
   A returns_nonnull attribute is supported by compilers, so the name
   fits, but it diverges from the existing naming convention in
   include/linux/compiler_attributes.h.

3. #define __nonnull(params) __attribute__((__nonnull__ params)). This
   keeps the name and is compatible with both kernel and glibc usage.
   Current call sites use __nonnull() with no arguments, which works
   identically. Future callers with specific parameter numbers would use
   __nonnull((1, 2)) with double parens, matching glibc's convention. I
   don't like this option, listed it here for the sake of completeness.

I am leaning towards option 1 so far.

review_comments.author: Miguel Ojeda
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miguel Ojeda suggested standardizing the spelling of __nonnull() across the kernel by introducing a single, consistent naming convention for this attribute, such as just 'nonnull', '__knonnull', or other variations, to avoid confusion and make maintenance easier.
review_comments.raw_body:
:(

What about other similar names? i.e. a variation of your option 2,
e.g. just `nonnull` (we also have others like that, i.e. no
underscore, e.g. `noinline`), or `___nonnull` (triple underscore, but
may be confusing), or a suffix/prefix letter, e.g. `__knonnull` (for
kernel nonnull)...

i.e. it would be nice to have a "standard" spelling for ourselves, and
also replace the existing `__attribute__((nonnull))`s we have
elsewhere in the tree.

Cheers,
Miguel

series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon where the NFSD4_CALLBACK_RUNNING bit is set too early, preventing recall-any dispatch for affected clients. The fix involves moving the test_and_set_bit() operation to after checking the rate limit and callback state gates. This ensures that the bit is only taken when nfsd4_run_cb() will be called.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] nfsd: clear CALLBACK_RUNNING on failed delegation recall queue
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon where a callback is left running even after a recall queue attempt fails, causing subsequent break_lease operations to silently skip the recall and resulting in stalled conflicting opens or locks. The fix involves clearing the CALLBACK_RUNNING bit when the recall queue attempt fails, ensuring that the delegation can be broken as expected.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching the next request from sending it, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. It removes duplicated code in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and introduces a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the logic for handling requests and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging, making the code more clear and maintainable. The patch modifies several functions that interact with the ring, replacing direct calls to copy_to_user() with calls to the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), which makes it clearer and more centralized, and also handles errors and rate-limited logging. This change is part of a larger series of patches to improve the io-uring functionality in the fuse module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE implementation of dev_uring by introducing a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state`. This function encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. The existing code is simplified and made more modular, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The patch modifies several functions in the fuse module to use these new constants instead of hardcoded indices. This change improves code readability and maintainability by eliminating magic numbers and making it easier to understand the purpose of each index.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools in the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of payload buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first sqe. The kernel manages buffer selection and recycling using a buffer ring data structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for using registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use the same pinned pages for subsequent I/O operations and reducing overhead. Registered buffers require enabling the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag and setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag during registration. The patch also includes benchmark results showing a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and other improvements for different types of reads.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the fuse io-uring implementation in the Linux kernel. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for read/write operations by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities and is gated behind these requirements. The zero-copy write path involves registering relevant pages in the server's io-uring registered buffer table, operating on these pages using fixed buffer operations, and unregistering them when the request completes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module for using buffer pools and zero-copy functionality with io-uring. The documentation explains how to use buffer pools, which decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage. It also describes how the server manages the buffer pool through a buffer ring, enabling features like incremental buffer consumption and multiple buffer sizes per ring. This patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary copies of data between kernel and user space.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy functionality to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. It also allows for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The changes are based on registered buffers and zero-copy, which can provide significant throughput improvements depending on the server's per-request latency and backing I/O characteristics.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Bernd's concern about registering ring entries without payloads, explaining that entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less buffers than the number of entries, and that it would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer concurrently. The author also mentioned that supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring and some requests not needing payload buffers justify decoupling entries from buffers.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 3:49 PM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
>
> On 5/23/26 00:38, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 2:51 PM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
> >>
> >> One piece I had been pondering a bit about, though did not dive into the
> >> code yet.
> >> What is if payload would be registered per ring entry (as without your
> >> changes) and just the header would become a buffer ring (so that could
> >
> > I think payload buffers must be decoupled from ring entries if we want
> > to reduce memory usage in the non-zero-copy path.
> >
> >> be pinned that way without loosing one page per entry)?  For zero-copy
> >
> > For the header pinning changes in my branch, the headers are one
> > contiguous region (not per-entry), which circumvents having to
> > allocate one page per entry.
> >
> >> we could register entries without a payload? Advantage here would be
> >> that that header buffer would determine max number of entries, only
> >> little memory would be needed for over-subscribed entries, but entries
> >> themselves could be registered dynamically on demand.
> >
> > For zero-copy, entries still may have payloads (though the buffers
> > will definitely not need to be as big as the max payload size!),
> > either for the non-zero-copyable args or if the request's args aren't
> > page-backed. Unfortunately I think many servers may not be able to use
> > zero-copy due to the CAP_SYS_ADMIN requirements, so I think it'd be
> > ideal to minimize the memory consumption needed for the non-zero-copy
> > path, through the buffer pool for the payload buffers.
>
> Well, my problem is still why would we need to register ring entries if
> they don't have buffers - why not just register less ring entries then.

Entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less
buffers than the number of entries. Most requests don't use the full
payload buffer size, so for example with incremental buffer
consumption, this would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping
regions of the same buffer concurrently. As well, if we want to
support multiple buffer sizes per ring, there shouldn't be a 1:1
mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that
don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,
etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer
mapping.

Thanks,

guesstimate this as it doesn't know the request size until it sees the
request. The server could speculatively pre-register ring ents ahead

which there is not an non-messy way to do (for buffer pools, the pool

Please take a look at patch 8 ([PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support

is contiguously allocated and registered as an io-uring registered
buffer, and then passed to fuse and by that point, the buffers have

purpose of this series is to decouple the 1:1 entry:buffer mapping. Eg

That is orthogonal to this series. This series focuses on payload
buffers, and header pinning is not part of this series as
bufpools/bufrings do not need pinned headers, as it makes no
difference for performance. As mentioned in the previous message, when
I was working on a separate series for removing the dispatch overhead,
I implemented header pinning as part of that, as that is when header
pinning becomes actually useful.

I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.

This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.

This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing
multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) and supporting multi-size buffers per ring.

Thanks,
Joanne

It's not just about the memory savings. Fundamentally, I believe it's
the wrong abstraction to statically couple ents to buffers 1:1 as a
fixed mapping. Entries and buffers serve different purposes. Entries

particular buffer size (eg the added ent can be used to service *any*
request size, not just the size of the buffer it's tied to). With
tying ents to buffers, the server is stuck with a static distribution
that can't adapt to workload changes.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Bernd Schubert questioned whether header pinning is still possible, noting that if it's not done, io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, resulting in a context switch for sync requests. He also proposed an alternative approach where the payload would be registered per ring entry and only the header would become a buffer ring.

Hi Joanne,

On 5/22/26 23:14, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59\u202fPM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

>> v2 -> v3:
>> * Rework the uapis to be more ergonomic. Use io-uring registered buffers
>>   infrastructure instead of doing pinning logic in fuse. Get rid of header
>>   pinning as that makes no real perf difference
>> * Rename from "buffer ring" to buffer pool. Logic is the same, just different
>>   naming

short question, I didn't check the code is header pinning still
*possible*? The problem if we do not pin headers is that
io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() cannot be avoided, as the application
cannot write into fuse-server buffers and headers without pre-pinned
pages. And io_uring_cmd_complete_in_task() basically means a context
switch for all sync requests.

> 
> Just to reiterate, the logic is the same between this version and the
> previous versions. I just renamed it to "buffer pool" to decouple the
> implementation detail from the concept since it's uapi facing. The
> buffer pool is managed as a buffer ring internally.
> 
> Bernd, it would be much appreciated if you could take a look at this
> series when you have the time. I'm targeting the June merge window and
> the io-uring dependent changes have already been queued up in Jens's
> tree since the last merge window.

I will try and right is probably a good time, almost through with
libfuse sync-init (remaining are bugs), Darricks series is also almost
ready. Although I don't promise this weekend - reserved for Darrick and
other libfuse changes.

One piece I had been pondering a bit about, though did not dive into the
code yet.
What is if payload would be registered per ring entry (as without your
changes) and just the header would become a buffer ring (so that could
be pinned that way without loosing one page per entry)?  For zero-copy
we could register entries without a payload? Advantage here would be
that that header buffer would determine max number of entries, only
little memory would be needed for over-subscribed entries, but entries
themselves could be registered dynamically on demand.


Thanks,
Bernd

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] fuse: re-lock request before returning from fuse_ref_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free issue in the fuse kernel module by re-locking the request before returning from the fuse_ref_folio() function, which unlocks the request but does not re-lock it. This ensures that the subsequent copy chain logic can safely access the request's arguments without encountering memory corruption issues. The fix is applied to the fs/fuse/dev.c file and addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch (c3021629a0d8) that added support for splice() reading from fuse devices.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a use-after-free issue in the fuse kernel module by re-locking the request before replacing the page cache folio. The problem arises when the subsequent copy chain logic accesses the fuse_io_args after it has been freed. The fix involves calling lock_request() before replace_page_cache_folio() to ensure the request is locked on the success path.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addresses Bernd Schubert's concern that statically coupling entries to buffers 1:1 as a fixed mapping introduces unnecessary complexity and inflexibility, by explaining that this series decouples the concepts of entries and buffers, allowing for dynamic addition of generic entries not tied to a particular buffer size.

Author responded to concerns about the need for dynamic buffer allocation, explaining that buffer pools decouple entries from specific sizes, making them generic and able to service any size request.

Author clarified that buffer pools in the patch series are designed to decouple buffers from entries, enabling incremental consumption and multi-size buffers as future work, not just a special case for zero-payload requests.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's not just about the memory savings. Fundamentally, I believe it's
the wrong abstraction to statically couple ents to buffers 1:1 as a
fixed mapping. Entries and buffers serve different purposes. Entries
handle request flow, buffers hold data. Statically coupling them 1:1
is inflexible, introduces unnecessary complexity, and imo has no added
benefit. This series decouples these two concepts. It allows
dynamically adding ents in the future as *generic* ents not tied to a
particular buffer size (eg the added ent can be used to service *any*
request size, not just the size of the buffer it's tied to). With
tying ents to buffers, the server is stuck with a static distribution
that can't adapt to workload changes.

---

Buffer pools / buffer rings make entries generic (can service any
size), whereas per-entry different sizes locks each entry to a
specific size. I don't think ents and buffers should be statically
coupled 1:1.

---

I think we're talking past each other. Buffer pools aren't about "zero
payload" as a special case - they're about decoupling buffers from
entries in general. Zero-payload requests are one beneficiary, but the
design enables incremental consumption and multi-size buffers as
future work precisely because of this decoupling.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 8/8] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups in a suboptimal fashion by avoiding an imperfect guard rail that splits large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used widely by other major shrinkers.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that deferred_split_folio() can set the shrinker bit on the wrong memcg if the original memcg is dying and already reparented, and suggested that __list_lru_add() should use the parent memcg instead of the original one.

The reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where an allocation fails and suggested setting the result to SCAN_ALLOC_HUGE_PAGE_FAIL.
review_comments.raw_body:
Can the shrinker bit end up on the wrong memcg here?

deferred_split_folio() takes the lock via list_lru_lock_irqsave() with
the original folio_memcg() of the folio. If that memcg is dying and
already reparented, lock_list_lru_of_memcg() walks up parent_mem_cgroup()
until it finds a live sublist and locks it (lru), but the memcg local
variable still points at the dying child.

__list_lru_add() then calls set_shrinker_bit(memcg, ...) with the
original (dying / reparented) memcg, not the parent that actually owns
the locked sublist where the folio was queued.

---

Over here, you will end up reporting success on allocation failure.

Maybe set result to SCAN_ALLOC_HUGE_PAGE_FAIL?

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: optimize stock usage for cgroup v2
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory usage in cgroup v2 by draining the stock of parent cgroups when a child is created, allowing other memcgs to reclaim the pages. This is necessary because direct charges to non-leaf cgroups are rare in cgroup v2, resulting in wasted memory in parent cgroups.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/7 v3] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the memcg (memory cgroup) code to use page_counter_stock instead of its own stock handling logic. This change removes special casing for avoiding refilling and flushing of old stock, allows for failure during page_counter_enable_stock() if there is not enough memory to allocate a percpu page_counter_stock, and restructures drain_all_stock to iterate CPUs in the outer loop for better performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/7 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce stock drain APIs
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces two new APIs, page_counter_drain_stock_local() and page_counter_drain_stock_cpu(), to replace existing memcg stock draining functions. The local version takes a lock to serialize against concurrent charges, while the CPU version does not take a lock as it will only be called from the CPU hotplug path where no more charges can occur. These new APIs are designed to drain the stock of a page counter in a thread-safe manner.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/7 v3] mm/page_counter: use page_counter_stock in page_counter_try_charge
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the page_counter_try_charge function to be aware of stock limits. It first checks if there is enough stock available, and if so, charges from the stock without attempting a hierarchical charge. If there is not enough stock, it attempts a greedy hierarchical charge and then tries to add any surplus pages to the stock. The patch preserves the existing semantics for handling stock in try_charge_memcg.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/7 v3] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock to cache pre-charged pages, addressing issues with the current implementation's random eviction and tight coupling between stock and memcg. The new design allows for separate stock caches for each page counter, reducing latency and thrashing.

review_comments.author: Sashiko
review_comments.summary: Raised a concern about racy charges and suggested reordering calls to prevent double-uncharging. Proposed a fix for the disable_stock function.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged Sashiko's concern and proposed a fix for the disable_stock function, reordering calls to prevent double-uncharging.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 0/7 v3] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter, consolidating stock limit checking and page_counter limit checking into page_counter_try_charge. This eliminates the 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit, random evictions (drain & refill), and slot traversal. The resulting code in memcg is also easier to follow, as the caching becomes transparent from memcg's perspective and managed entirely within page_counter.

review_comments.author: Sashiko
review_comments.summary: Raised a concern about racy charges and double-uncharging when racing with concurrent charges. Suggested reordering the callsite to prevent this issue.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Addressed Sashiko's concerns about racy charges and double-uncharging, explaining that reordering the callsite can prevent this issue.

review_comments.author: Sashiko
review_comments.summary: Raised additional concerns about synchronous draining with the percpu_charge_mutex lock held, potential ABBA deadlock with mmap_lock, and NOHZ / CPU isolation.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn
review_comments.summary: Addressed Sashiko's concerns about synchronous draining, explaining that it is not a significant issue. Also acknowledged the potential ABBA deadlock and NOHZ / CPU isolation issues.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the memsw stock for cgroup v1 by introducing an independent stock for the memsw page counter, which is charged separately from the memory page counter. This change improves caching and reduces the worst-case scenario where both counters exceed their limits, requiring a hierarchical charge. The user-visible effect is that reported memsw usage may temporarily be lower than memory usage due to batching of stock charges.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/7 v3] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/compaction: cap compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/compaction: cap compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the value returned by compact_gap() to prevent over-reserving memory for compaction, which can lead to unnecessary reclaim and failed compaction suitability checks. The current implementation returns a gap that scales exponentially with order, but is bounded by COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX. By capping this value at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX, the patch aims to improve compaction success rates and reduce unnecessary reclaim on fragmented production hosts.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX would lead to unnecessary reclamation due to genuine memory pressure, suggesting that processes may continuously allocate and reclaim without the gap

Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether the reduction in reclaim is due to increased allocation size or a shift to direct reclaim, requesting clarification on the actual cause.

Reviewer Vlastimil expressed skepticism about the patch's A/B test results, questioning why the free memory would suddenly stabilize and improve significantly after capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX.
sentiment_signals: raised a concern about potential performance impact
review_comments.raw_body:
But doesn't that mean there's genuine memory pressure? We're effectively
raising the high watermark by 4 MB, but if processes are continuously
allocating, we'd be reclaiming without the gap as well? Unless the workload
is sized to fit without the gap.

---

Did the extra reclaim just disappear because we allow the allocations to use
4MB more memory? Or it shifted to direct reclaim?

---

Weird that things would improve that much. I would expect the free memory
just to stabilize around the lower gap but then behave similarly. Are we
missing something here?

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that order-9 THP failures were waking up kswapd and identified compact_gap() as a contributing factor, but did not indicate plans for further revision.

Author acknowledged that the patch's performance improvements are consistent across different kernel versions and builds, but did not address any specific technical concerns or provide a clear resolution to previous feedback.

Author addresses Vlastimil's concern about the impact of capping compact_gap() on order-9 THP allocations, explaining that it allows compaction to take over sooner and start working to produce large size pages needed for THP.

Author acknowledged that bursty network allocations can reserve many pageblocks as high atomic, which affects the patch's assumptions about THP-size pages becoming eligible for allocation.

Author is addressing concerns about over-reserving compact_gap() space, proposes to refactor patch to make it reclaim-focused and use a separate function for non-reclaim users.
sentiment_signals: did not commit to fixing it
author provided additional information but no direct response to prior feedback
review_comments.raw_body:
It wasn't actual pressure, but the repetitive order-9 THP failures that were
waking up kswapd. I should make this more clear in the changelog. After
looking into why so much reclaim was occurring though, the compact gap stood
out since it dictates the target amount to reclaim.

---

6.13. Additional benchmarks were done using a recent mm-new build as well,
and they showed similar reductions in reclaim.

---

Specifically in the order-9 case, the reclaim target goes from 1024 to 32.
What the data shows is that capping the gap allows compaction to take over
sooner and start working to produce large size pages needed for THP. Whereas
in the pre-patch state, trying to reclaim the full 2x THP delays compaction.

---

This patch was tested in isolation, but also occurring was the case where
bursty net allocations reserve many pageblocks as high atomic. So as
THP-size pages become eligible, their blocks are reserved before being
allocated as THP.

---

I'm thinking I could reframe this patch as reclaim-focused and use
min(2UL << order, COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX) as a reclaim-only target, while
either leaving the other non-reclaim users of this function alone or
using the 2x form you suggest above. i.e. I can split this function
into a separate reclaim_compact_gap() and use the originally proposed cap.
Thoughts?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: skip high atomic reservation at or below costly order

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: skip high atomic reservation at or below costly order

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to prevent high atomic reservations for allocations at or below a certain order (PAGE_ALLOC_COSTLY_ORDER). This is done by adding a check in the reserve_highatomic_pageblock function, which skips reserving entire pageblocks for smaller atomic allocations. The goal of this change is to reduce fragmentation and improve the success rate of large memory allocations, particularly for Transparent Huge Pages (THP) on systems with plenty of free memory.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Andrew Morton questioned whether skipping HighAtomic reservation for orders 1-3 would break anti-fragmentation guarantees, potentially leading to dropped packets or I/O errors in subsystems like the network stack or BTRFS.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko asked

: Does skipping the HighAtomic reservation for orders 1-3 break the
: anti-fragmentation guarantees for these atomic allocations?
: 
: The MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC reserve protects high-order atomic allocations
: from failing under fragmentation by taking ownership of the entire
: pageblock.
: 
: If order-1 through order-3 atomic allocations fall back to stealing
: pages, but the pageblock remains in its original migratetype, won't
: order-0 non-atomic allocations consume the remaining contiguous space?
: 
: Under memory pressure, this could leave no contiguous blocks for atomic
: allocations to steal.  Because these atomic allocations cannot trigger
: direct reclaim or compaction, they might fail, potentially leading to
: dropped packets or I/O errors in subsystems like the network stack or
: BTRFS.
: 
: Could background compaction or khugepaged be used to unreserve
: HighAtomic blocks dynamically instead of disabling the reserve for
: these orders?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Johannes Weiner questioned the patch's effectiveness, noting that disabling highatomic reserve for costly orders didn't seem to matter and suggesting that maxing out reserves is unnecessary as highatomic allocations are typically short-lived.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is an interesting patch. A couple of thoughts:

1. You disabled the highatomic reserve for this workload and it didn't
seem to matter. Presumably <costly orders don't need the protection.

2. Maxing out the reserves is odd. ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC allocations will
try reserved space first, and I'd expect things that are commonly
highatomic to be short-lived. Why don't we stop with a couple of
claimed highatomic blocks that get continuously recycled?

3. The impact on THP and compaction success rate is pretty
extreme. How can 1% of memory throw such a wrench into the gears?

Have you tried this with other workloads?

review_comments.author: JP (Meta) (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged reviewer's feedback that smaller atomic allocations do not benefit from the high atomic reservation, agreeing that it should be skipped for orders 1-3.

Author acknowledges that smaller atomic allocations do not reserve entire pageblocks, as observed in experiments where there were no failures for orders 1-3 on a fragmented system.

Author addressed Andrew Morton's concern that the patch would prevent smaller atomic allocations from being movable, explaining that the patch actually keeps these pageblocks movable and improves compaction

Author addressed a concern that reserved HighAtomic pageblocks are not treated as precious resources, explaining that under real memory pressure they are already given up by the kernel's unreserve mechanism.

Author responded to a concern about introducing additional overhead by explaining that the current patch already reduces reclaim and arguing against adding more scanning/overhead/stats.
review_comments.raw_body:
The data included in the changelog supports the claim that the reserve
does not provide a benefit at these orders. Even on fragmented systems,
orders 1-3 have plenty of pages available.

---

In the experiments, there were no failures for orders 1-3 despite a
fragmented system and no reserved pageblocks for these orders.

---

With the patch, these pageblocks stay movable. So if fallback is needed,
moveable pages can still be taken. But the patch actually improves
compaction so contiguous space is increased overall.

---

Reserved HighAtomic pageblocks are not currently treated as a precious
resource. Under real memory pressure, the kernel already gives them up -
the unreserve mechanism kicks in and converts the HighAtomic pageblocks
back to their original migrate type.

---

This would call for extra scanning/overhead/stats. The patch reduces
reclaim, for example. A new scanner feels like going in the opposite
direction.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil pointed out that the patch's logic for reserving high atomic pageblocks may not work as intended when an allocation succeeds before entering the slowpath, potentially leading to unnecessary reservation of additional blocks.

Reviewer Vlastimil suggested that instead of skipping high atomic reservations for low orders, the patch should focus on unreserving these reservations more proactively.

Reviewer noted that if all free memory is in HighAtomic blocks, compaction may not be effective and suggested a suitability check might be incorrectly assuming these blocks are usable
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm, but if the allocation succeeds before entering slowpath,
ALLOC_NON_BLOCK won't be set.
But reserving another block should mean we already exhausted the reserved ones.
Unreserving is only done when direct reclaim made some progress but failed
to produce a page. But if it works, or kswapd does the job, we won't enter it?

---

Maybe it's some big burst of highatomic allocations that leads to the
reservations and then they stay around "forever"?

If that's the case I think we should be perhaps looking at the unreserving
being done more proactively, rather than limiting things to costly order.

---

Maybe if ~all free memory is in the highatomic blocks, compaction can't be
effective much. Or some suitability check somewhere in reclaim+compaction
wrongly assumes the highatomic blocks are usable, so it won't do the work.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges a potential configuration issue and plans to address it by considering pageblock_order in the patch

Author acknowledged that smaller atomic allocations can still lead to high reservation rates due to steady allocation volumes and agreed to reconsider the patch's approach.

Author acknowledges that the patch improves the situation by making high atomic pageblocks available for THP allocations, and notes that these blocks were previously unusable.

Author acknowledged that the patch's benefits may not be immediately apparent on workloads with frequent network allocations, and noted that memory size is also a factor.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right. Although one detail I realize is I should also consider
pageblock_order as well to avoid any config issue.

---

Even though they may be short-lived, the data shows the volume of
allocations is steady enough to keep the reserves maxed out.

---

Looking at the pre-patched high atomic pageblock counts, that's ~300
pageblocks that could've been used for THPs. They become usable after
the patch.

---

No, but the pre-patch symptoms will show up on workloads where net
allocs are frequent enough to keep the high atomic pageblock count up.
Memory size of hosts involved is a factor as well since it's possible for a
majority of order-9 pages to be stuck in high atomic.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that there is no need for the patch's logic to handle the case where the high atomic reservation is not invoked, and awaits further clarification from the reviewer.

Author acknowledges a concern about high atomic reservation at low orders and explains that high frequency net allocations contribute to this issue, promising to add this information to the changelog.

Author is questioning whether reserving full pageblocks for small allocations is necessary, suggesting that coalescing in the buddy path might be sufficient based on the provided data.

Author JP Kobryn addressed a concern about whether the patch's approach to preventing high atomic reservation at or below costly order is correct, explaining that free highatomic pages are removed from usable free count in __zone_watermark_ok() and compaction suitability path, and that fragmentation accounting may be skewed. Author believes the patch prevents the problem up front.
review_comments.raw_body:
There was just no real pressure to invoke the unreserving. Let me know
if I'm misunderstanding the question.

---

I should add to the changelog the missing info that high frequency
net allocations are responsible for these high atomic reservations.
Even though the allocations are not necessarily long-lived, the
pageblocks remain high atomic.

---

What are your thoughts if we instead look at it as: should we be reserving
full pageblocks for small allocations?

It seems to come down to whether we want the disproportionate protection 
of full
pageblocks (below costly order) for high atomic allocs vs letting them 
coalesce
in the buddy path. Is the data not enough to justify the latter?

---

I could be missing something, but I spent some time tonight looking into
this and didn't find an issue in the compaction/reclaim suitability path.

__compaction_suitable() calls __zone_watermark_ok(), and that path
subtracts free MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC pages from usable free memory for
callers without reserve access:

 /*
  * If the caller does not have rights to reserves below the min
  * watermark then subtract the free pages reserved for highatomic.
  */
 if (likely(!(alloc_flags & ALLOC_RESERVES)))
   unusable_free += READ_ONCE(z->nr_free_highatomic);

So free highatomic pages are removed from the usable free count there.

Also, the suitable-free-block check in __zone_watermark_ok() only treats
MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC as usable when alloc_flags includes
ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC (or ALLOC_OOM). __compaction_suitable() passes
ALLOC_CMA here (not ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC), so I don't think compaction is
incorrectly treating free highatomic blocks as usable.

The only caveat I noticed is the fragmentation accounting side:
fill_contig_page_info() / fragmentation_index() appear to count
free_area[order].nr_free across migratetypes, so fragmentation scoring
may look better than they really are. But that seems adjacent
to this patch.

I think though that by the time we consider reclaim or compaction we're
dealing with the aftermath. The patch prevents the problem from occurring
up front.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH man-pages v2 0/6] userfaultfd: document read-write-protect mode

version_history.subject: [PATCH man-pages v2 0/6] userfaultfd: document read-write-protect mode

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/6] userfaultfd.2: Add read-write-protect mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write-protect mode to the userfaultfd system call, allowing it to trap every access (read or write) to a present page within a registered range. The new feature is documented in the man pages and includes several new ioctl commands and flags. It's intended for use by virtual memory managers and similar callers to track working sets and evict cold pages. The patch also adds support for asynchronous fault resolution, where the kernel auto-restores page permissions on fault without delivering a notification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/6] UFFDIO_RWPROTECT.2const: New page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for the UFFDIO_RWPROTECT ioctl, which installs or removes read-write protection on a userfaultfd-registered memory range. The ioctl supports two mode bits: UFFDIO_RWPROTECT_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT_MODE_DONTWAKE. It also covers the behavior of read-write protection for populated pages only, anonymous vs file-backed reclaim, and error handling. The patch introduces a new man page (UFFDIO_RWPROTECT.2const) to document this ioctl.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/6] UFFDIO_SET_MODE.2const: New page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for the UFFDIO_SET_MODE ioctl, which toggles userfaultfd feature bits at runtime. The ioctl takes a struct uffdio_set_mode as an argument, which contains two fields: enable and disable. These fields specify the features to be turned on or off, respectively. Currently, only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC is toggleable, and enabling it requires UFFD_FEATURE_RWP to have been negotiated at UFFDIO_API time. The operation is serialized against in-flight page faults, allowing a single userfaultfd to switch between async detection and synchronous eviction without re-registering its ranges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/6] UFFDIO_API.2const: Document UFFD_FEATURE_RWP{,_ASYNC} and 1 << _UFFDIO_SET_MODE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for two new feature bits introduced in Linux 7.2: UFFD_FEATURE_RWP and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which enable read-write-protection tracking and in-kernel resolution of RWP faults without notification. It also documents the file-descriptor-level bit 1 << _UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which advertises support for toggling UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC at runtime using the UFFDIO_SET_MODE ioctl.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/6] UFFDIO_REGISTER.2const: Document UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and 1 << _UFFDIO_RWPROTECT
series_items.patch_summary: This patch documents the new UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP registration mode and its associated bit, 1 << _UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which allows tracking of every access to a registered page range. The documentation also clarifies compatibility with anonymous, shmem, and hugetlbfs ranges. The patch adds descriptions for these features in the UFFDIO_REGISTER manual page.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/6] ioctl_userfaultfd.2: Reference UFFDIO_RWPROTECT and UFFDIO_SET_MODE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new ioctls, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT and UFFDIO_SET_MODE, to the list of operations supported on a userfaultfd file descriptor in the Linux kernel documentation.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series documents the userfaultfd read-write-protect mode, a working-set tracking mechanism for VM guest memory in Linux 7.2. The patches add new documentation to various man pages, including userfaultfd.2, UFFDIO_API.2const, and ioctl_userfaultfd.2, to describe the read-write-protect mode, its registration, and associated ioctls. The changes address review comments and improve formatting and consistency throughout the documentation.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 00/18] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/16] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory
[PATCH v4 00/14] userfaultfd: working set tracking for VM guest memory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/18] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() prot-update race
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the make_uffd_wp_huge_pte() function, which is responsible for setting the UFFD_WP bit on present HugeTLB PTEs. The issue arises when the kernel-owned snapshot of the PTE is not updated atomically with the hardware update, leading to stale data being written back over the live hardware bits. To fix this, the patch mirrors the pattern used in the non-hugetlb sibling make_uffd_wp_pte() function by using ptep_modify_prot_start() and ptep_modify_prot_commit(). This ensures that the PTE is updated atomically, preventing the race condition and ensuring data consistency.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/18] mm/huge_memory: preserve pmd_swp_uffd_wp on device-private PMD downgrade
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's huge memory handling code, specifically when downgrading a device-private PMD (Page Map Directory) to a non-device-private one. The issue was that the pmd_swp_uffd_wp bit was being lost during this downgrade process, which could cause UFFD_WP (User Fault on File Descriptor Write Protection) traps to be bypassed. To fix this, the patch adds code to preserve the pmd_swp_uffd_wp bit when downgrading a device-private PMD.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/18] userfaultfd: gate must_wait writability check on pte_present()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the userfaultfd code to gate the writability check on pte_present() when checking if a fault still requires userspace handling due to write protection (WP). This prevents incorrect results from non-marker swap entries, which can occur during concurrent migration or swap-out. The change ensures that the lockless re-check only inspects present PTE bits when the entry is actually present, preventing potential deadlocks and ensuring correct behavior in such scenarios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/18] mm: skip out-of-range bits in mk_vma_flags()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel where passing out-of-range bits to the mk_vma_flags() function could silently add extra bits to the mask, potentially causing issues. The patch introduces VMA_NO_BIT as a special value that is used when a bit number is out of range, and modifies the DECLARE_VMA_BIT macro to use this value for invalid bit numbers. This ensures that only valid bits are added to the mask, preventing potential problems.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/18] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to use them independently of NUMA balancing. The six architectures currently supporting protnone PTEs select this option and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it. No functional changes are made, but the infrastructure is now available separately from NUMA balancing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/18] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' across various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The change is purely mechanical and does not alter any behavior. This is done in preparation for userfaultfd RWP (Read-Write Protection) to use the same bit to mark access-tracking PTEs, distinct from mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA-hinting PTEs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/18] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel-internal functions and variables related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is reused for marking access-tracking PTEs in addition to write protection. The renaming is mechanical and does not change behavior. The changes affect various architecture-specific files as well as core mm code. The goal of this patch is to simplify the kernel's handling of userfaultfd by making its internal names consistent with its new usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/18] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag, VM_UFFD_RWP, to enable userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP extends the existing write-protection mechanism to also trap reads on protected ranges. The patch introduces the necessary infrastructure, including helpers and documentation updates, but does not yet set or query the flag. It is a preparatory patch for the rest of the series, which will implement userfaultfd read-write protection.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/18] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function in the Linux kernel: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. These flags are used for userfaultfd (UFFD) read-write protection, allowing UFFD to use a special PTE bit to distinguish faults from mprotect(PROT_NONE) or NUMA hinting faults. The patch also updates various kernel functions to handle these new flags correctly, ensuring that anon, shmem, THP, and hugetlb all share the same semantics for UFFD RWP protection.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/18] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. Without this preservation, the RWP tracking could be silently dropped, leading to unexpected behavior when accessing pages with PAGE_NONE semantics. The patch adds checks and modifications to maintain the uffd bit (RWP marker) across these operations, ensuring that it is not lost during PTE rewrites.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/18] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three mm paths to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same as VM_UFFD_WP, ensuring that per-PTE RWP state is not silently destroyed or bypassed. The changes affect khugepaged, rmap, and GUP, which are modified to handle VM_UFFD_RWP ranges with the same protection as VM_UFFD_WP. This patch addresses a problem where userfaultfd RWP markers were being ignored in certain scenarios, leading to potential data corruption or security issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/18] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd (UFFD) system, which allows userspace to track read-write protection on memory ranges. It introduces two new UFFD ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, as well as a capability bit UFFD_FEATURE_RWP. The feature is not yet advertised in the UAPI, but will be exposed in a follow-up patch. The patch also includes changes to the kernel's configuration, documentation, and code to support this new feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/18] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for read-write protection (RWP) to the userfaultfd interface in the Linux kernel. It introduces a new fault delivery mechanism that triggers when a process attempts to access memory protected by RWP, and exposes a new UAPI command to register RWP mode. The patch also updates various kernel functions to handle RWP faults correctly. The goal is to provide userspace with a way to track and manage read-write protection for specific memory regions. The approach taken involves modifying the fault handling paths in the kernel to detect RWP-protected pages and dispatching UFFD_PAGEFAULT_FLAG_RWP messages through the userfaultfd interface. This allows userspace to resolve RWP faults using the UFFDIO_RWPROTECT command.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/18] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which is used for tracking read-write protection (RWP) in userfaultfd. The existing PTE bit is reused for both write-protected and RWP-protected pages, but this patch introduces a distinction between the two by scoping each to the relevant VMA. This change also updates the pagemap_hugetlb_category function to take into account the VMA when determining which category to use. The patch aims to provide a more accurate representation of page state and improve the userfaultfd interface.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/18] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults. This is useful for working-set tracking in virtual machines, where the kernel can restore access to pages without blocking the faulting thread. The VMM can then reconstruct the access pattern after the fact using PAGEMAP_SCAN. Additionally, this feature upgrades writable private anonymous PTEs when possible, preventing unnecessary copy-on-write faults.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/18] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This patch also includes changes to userfaultfd.c and include/uapi/linux/userfaultfd.h files.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/18] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd read-write-protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous modes, page mapping, memory protection, and fork operations. They verify that RWP works correctly in different situations, such as when pages are touched, accessed, or protected. The patch includes 11 test cases that run against anonymous, shared memory, and hugetlb memory types. It also ensures that the userfaultfd API returns the expected ioctls bitmask. The tests aim to provide comprehensive coverage for UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT functionality.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/18] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd feature, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP) working set tracking. It explains how RWP works, including its sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical workflow for VMM working-set-tracking from detection to eviction and back to async mode.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces working set tracking for VM guest memory, enabling userfaultfd to provide read-write protection (RWP) for virtual machine guests. The patches add new flags and functions to support RWP, including a VMA flag, change_protection() primitives, and modifications to the huge page handling code.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux man-pages for the userfaultfd system call, specifically for the read-write protect mode (UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP) introduced in Linux 7.2. The new feature allows user-space to receive page-fault notifications on any access (read or write) to a present page within a registered range, and provides options for asynchronous fault resolution. The patch updates the userfaultfd.2 man page to include descriptions of the new registration mode, pagefault flag, and VERSIONS line.

review_comments.author: Alejandro Colomar
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer pointed out that the description of UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP incorrectly states it traps every access to a present page, when in fact it only traps write accesses.

The reviewer requested that the patch include more useful hunk contexts, citing documentation in the CONTRIBUTING file and CONTRIBUTING.d directory.

The reviewer requested changes to the documentation formatting, specifically suggesting the use of \\[em] instead of \(em and semantic newlines to improve readability.

The reviewer pointed out two minor formatting issues in the man-page documentation: 'by default' should be followed by a comma, and the word 'until' should be moved to the next line due to semantic newlines.

The reviewer pointed out a minor grammar issue in the documentation, suggesting that 'user space' should be written as two words ('user-space') when used as a compound adjective.

The reviewer suggested reformatting a paragraph in the man-page documentation for better readability, specifically recommending that each sentence or clause should be on its own line to avoid ambiguity and improve comprehension.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar suggested changing 'returns' to 'fails with/' in the documentation to accurately reflect that attempting to register both UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_WP on the same range results in an error.

The reviewer suggested renaming the 'read-write protect' mode to either 'read-write-protect' or 'read/write-protect' for consistency with the existing 'write-protect' mode.

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar noted that the patch uses .TP instead of .P for paragraph formatting, which is inconsistent with the rest of the man page, and suggested switching back to .P

Reviewer Alejandro Colomar requested that the patch be reformatted to have only one identifier per line, citing consistency as the reason.

series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 2/6] UFFDIO_RWPROTECT.2const: New page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux man-pages project for the UFFDIO_RWPROTECT ioctl, which is used to install or remove read-write protection on a userfaultfd-registered memory range. series_items.subject: [PATCH man-pages v1 3/6] UFFDIO_SET_MODE.2const: New page
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new manual page for the UFFDIO_SET_MODE ioctl, which allows userfaultfd feature bits to be toggled at runtime. The patch documents the uffdio_set_mode struct and its serialization against in-flight page faults, enabling a single userfaultfd to switch between async detection and synchronous eviction without re-registering its ranges.

These features enable read-write protection tracking and in-kernel resolution of RWP faults without notification. The patch also documents the ability to toggle this asynchronous mode at runtime using the UFFDIO_SET_MODE(2const) ioctl.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] btrfs: allocate eb-attached btree pages as movable

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the way btrfs allocates pages for extent buffers to make them movable, which allows the page allocator to move these pages even when they are attached to a btree inode. The change involves passing the full GFP mask directly to functions that allocate pages, rather than having them calculate it from boolean flags. This is done to address fragmentation issues caused by btree_inode pages being pinned in unmovable pageblocks, which prevents hugepage allocation and leads to disk space waste. The patch targets a specific problem where ~28 GB of btree_inode pages are parked across many tainted superpageblocks on a test system, preventing 1 GiB hugepage allocation from those regions.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 00/40] mm: reliable 1GB page allocation

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 00/40] mm: reliable 1GB page allocation

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 01/40] mm: page_alloc: replace pageblock_flags bitmap with struct pageblock_data
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces the packed bitmap of pageblock flags with a per-pageblock struct containing its own flags word, reducing cache line touches in the free path and simplifying accessor code. The memory overhead is 8 bytes per pageblock, but this is considered negligible. No functional changes are made.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 02/40] mm: page_alloc: per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-cpu pageblock buddy allocator to improve scalability and reduce contention on large machines. The current zone->lock bottleneck is addressed by extending the per-CPU page cache (PCP) to operate on whole pageblocks, eliminating affinity violations and reducing lock hold times. The PCP refill process is divided into four phases: recovering owned fragments, claiming whole pageblocks, grabbing sub-pageblock chunks, and traditional __rmqueue() with migratetype fallback. This approach enables PCP-level merging, reduces fragmentation, and allows the allocator to degrade gracefully when necessary.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 03/40] mm: page_alloc: split-path PCP free with local-trylock + remote-llist
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a lock-inversion risk in the page allocator's PCP free path by introducing a per-CPU remote free list (llist) that allows remote CPUs to enqueue pages for free without taking the zone->lock. The local CPU then absorbs these queued pages back onto its PCP buddy lists at the next opportunity, eliminating the need for a trylock-fallback approach and reducing lock contention. This change also includes various other optimizations, such as memory hot-add lazy init and improved handling of page allocation on dead CPUs.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 04/40] mm: mm_init: fix zone assignment for pages in unavailable ranges
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Linux kernel's memory initialization code where pages in unavailable ranges are assigned to the wrong zone. The problem occurs when an unavailable range spans multiple zones, causing later page_zone() calls to return incorrect results. To fix this, the patch looks up the correct zone for each PFN in the hole during initialization, which has no performance impact since it only runs once at boot time.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 05/40] mm: page_alloc: remove watermark boost mechanism
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the watermark boost mechanism from the Linux kernel's page allocation code. The mechanism was introduced to react to fragmentation events at the pageblock granularity, but it has been found to cause issues in workloads with high memory availability. With the introduction of superpageblocks and anti-fragmentation enforcement at 1 GiB granularity, sub-pageblock fallbacks are no longer meaningful signals. The boost is also applied before the success path is decided, leading to unnecessary wakeups of kswapd even when free pages are abundant. The patch drops the entire mechanism, removing various functions, flags, and sysctls related to watermark boosting.

Reviewer noted that removing the watermark boost mechanism would also require updating the 'boost' field in /proc/zoneinfo and show_mem output, suggesting a similar change should be made for these as well.
